chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.back: An identifier that acts as an alternative name for an existing type. It doesn't create a new distinct type, just a synonym for an existing one.

cards.text: To create a type alias in modern C++, use: {{c1::using}} AliasName {{c2::=}} ExistingType;

cards.question: How do you create a type alias named Distance for type double?
cards.explanation: The using keyword creates a type alias. Now Distance can be used anywhere double can be used.

cards.text: The older C-style way to create type aliases uses the {{c1::typedef}} keyword: typedef double Distance;

cards.front: Why prefer using over typedef for type aliases?
cards.back:
using is preferred because:
  • More readable and consistent syntax
  • Works better with templates (can be templated)
  • Left-to-right reading order (name = type)
  • Recommended by modern C++ style guides

cards.explanation:
Type aliases improve code in several ways:
  • Make complex types easier to read and understand
  • Document the purpose/meaning of a type
  • Reduce typing for long type names
  • Allow easy changes to underlying types
  • Hide platform-specific implementation details
examples: using Vec2D = std::pair<double, double>;  // simplifies complex type
using Distance = double;  // can change to float easily

cards.front: What is the scope of a type alias?
cards.back:
Type aliases follow normal identifier scoping rules:
  • Block scope if defined in a function/block
  • Global scope if defined at file level
  • Can be defined in namespaces or classes
They're only visible within their scope.

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.explanation: Type aliases don't create distinct types - both Miles and Kilometers are just aliases for double. They're fully interchangeable, which can be both convenient and dangerous.

cards.text: Type aliases are {{c1::not}} distinct types - they're just {{c2::synonyms}} for existing types and are fully {{c3::interchangeable}}.

cards.front: What are the common naming conventions for type aliases?
cards.back:
Type aliases typically use:
  • PascalCase (most common): StudentID, Distance
  • Same naming as types they represent
  • Suffix '_t' for some standard library types: size_t
Choose a convention and be consistent.

cards.question: What's wrong with this use of type aliases?
cards.answer: Nothing technically wrong (compiles fine), but it's semantically unclear - mixing Length and Width types without clear intent.
cards.explanation: Since type aliases don't create distinct types, the compiler won't catch semantic errors like mixing incompatible units. Type aliases provide documentation but not type safety.

cards.front: How do type aliases help with platform-specific code?
cards.back: Type aliases can hide platform-specific implementation details. For example, std::size_t is a type alias for an unsigned integral type whose actual type (unsigned int, unsigned long, etc.) varies by platform.

cards.text: Modern C++ uses {{c1::using}} to create type aliases: using Distance {{c2::=}} double;
Type aliases are {{c1::not}} distinct types - they're just {{c2::alternative names}} for existing types.
